full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This past Thursday evening, we opened St. Martin's Chapel from 5 until 6:30 for folks<|9.00|> <|9.00|> to stop in for prayer and to light a candle, to just be present with their faith community<|18.00|> <|18.00|> in the wake of so much recent violence in our country.<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is the fourth time we have done this in my two years at St. John's, and sadly, we could have done it many more than four times.<|12.42|> <|15.08|> Sitting in the chapel was a balm for all who came in, and many of you have shared with us that you prayed along with us even if you couldn't be there physically.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was a balm, and it was also a feast for the senses.<|6.82|> <|8.14|> As we prayed for those who have died, for those who have been so desperate and misguided as to perpetuate violence,<|17.68|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As we prayed for our legislators and for what feels sometimes like a battle for the very<|7.90|> <|7.90|> soul of our country, we prayed in confidence and in protest.<|16.70|> <|16.70|> Protest against the rising tide of paranoia and fear.<|22.84|> <|22.84|> We prayed with the doors open.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We prayed with the doors open and our backs to the street, trusting against all evidence to the contrary that we were in a sanctuary.<|12.66|> <|14.04|> My friends, sometimes coming to church is a revolutionary act.<|20.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> With those doors open, the world came in and met us.<|6.92|> <|7.88|> It was a feast for the senses.<|10.48|> <|11.92|> You could hear the things you might expect, the creak of kneelers as the faithful pulled<|20.14|> <|20.14|> down those ancient kneelers in St. Martin's and knelt in prayer.<|27.04|> <|27.04|> Amen.<|27.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You could hear the bass line of a passing car's stereo and the rattle of a ladder-laden work truck.<|8.74|> <|9.90|> You could hear a coach's whistle and shouts of encouragement.<|14.48|> <|15.80|> There's a back-to-school picnic happening in the lot at Morrie, where many of you may have parked this morning.<|22.40|> <|22.40|> There's a back-to-school picnic happening in the lot at Morrie, where many of you may have parked this morning.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You could hear children's voices calling out to friends they hadn't seen all summer.<|6.80|> <|8.54|> And you could see the familiar faces of the faithful who worship with us week in and week out—<|16.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and some unfamiliar faces. Some may have read about the prayer time on Facebook<|7.64|> <|7.64|> or simply walked past and been curious about open doors. And always, I'm entranced in<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> by those faces of the angels bowed in prayer and worship that surround Mary and the infant Jesus above the altar.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> And you could smell the exhaust of cars on a warm and still Denver evening.<|20.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> wonderfully mixed with the scent of candles being lighted by the faithful and burning in a votive stand.<|9.04|> <|10.64|> It's hard in St. Martin's and in here, in the cathedral, not to be aware and in awe of the decades of prayers that have been said in these holy spaces.<|25.98|> <|25.98|> Amen.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The faithful have gone to their knees in lament, in fear, in gratitude, and in hope for over 100 years in this space.<|12.46|> <|13.68|> Our prayers always join theirs.<|17.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The writer of the letter to the Hebrews saw this same connection, reminding those to whom<|8.52|> <|8.52|> he wrote that they were connected to a long history of faith, reminding them of the faith<|16.10|> <|16.10|> of all who came before them and helped them accomplish what they needed.<|23.58|> <|23.58|> Amen.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> By faith, the Israelites passed through the Red Sea.<|4.02|> <|4.88|> By faith, the walls of Jericho fell.<|7.94|> <|8.70|> By faith, Rahab did not perish.<|11.64|> <|12.42|> Kingdoms were conquered.<|13.54|> <|14.12|> Justice was administered.<|15.38|> <|16.08|> Lions were pacified.<|17.64|> <|18.56|> Strength came out of weakness.<|20.48|> <|21.40|> All by faith.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> By faith, we came together in prayer on Thursday.<|5.54|> <|6.68|> The passage goes on to list other faithful who suffered, were mocked, flogged, and martyred.<|15.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What I appreciate about this text from Hebrews is that it doesn't just say that if only we have more faith, we can do anything and everything, that life will be simple.<|12.44|> <|13.38|> It is not a prosperity gospel kind of message.<|17.96|> <|19.10|> It also does not glamorize a life of suffering.<|24.02|> <|25.10|> Simply stated,<|26.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Life can be difficult, and a Christian life is no exception.<|6.32|> <|7.64|> But a Christian life is lived by faith.<|12.34|> <|13.70|> The writer of Hebrews then shares some thoughts on how to live by faith.<|20.32|> <|20.40|> I always appreciate instructions.<|22.26|> <|22.26|> I always appreciate instructions.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Recall who surrounds us, that great cloud of witnesses made up of biblical giants mentioned<|10.44|> <|10.44|> in this scripture, portrayed in these beautiful windows and even in the carvings behind me<|18.56|> <|18.56|> at the high altar.<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are never praying alone.<|4.56|> <|5.52|> We have this rich history,<|7.54|> <|8.02|> the decades of witnesses who have prayed in this very space<|11.34|> <|11.34|> and who are praying around you right now.<|15.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Remove what weighs us down.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> Trust that by faith we are forgiven, redeemed, and renewed, and live as if we believe that.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> Rely on strength that is within us.<|18.00|> <|18.00|> Run with perseverance the race that is set before us.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And always remember who goes before us, looking to Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of our faith.<|12.26|> <|14.76|> As Broderick has stated in a previous sermon, he's not a fan of sports analogies, and I'm with him on that.<|21.80|> <|21.80|> Amen.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I read something that fascinated me about thinking about the race in the context of this<|9.38|> <|9.38|> passage and others by Paul that make a similar connection between living a life of faith and<|17.42|> <|17.42|> running a race.<|18.20|> <|18.20|> Amen.<|18.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There is perhaps no other sporting event that allows all level of athletes on the same course,<|9.34|> <|9.98|> at the same time, in front of the same cheering crowds.<|14.84|> <|16.12|> A road race can have a world record holder and a relative novice competing at the same time.<|24.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The corollary that was offered was that none of us would ever expect to walk onto a tennis court with Serena Williams.<|8.30|> <|11.42|> We all run the same course, whether new Christians or lifelong.<|18.94|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are all surrounded by that same great cloud of witnesses, and we are all looking to Jesus.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> We are all doing our personal best to live by faith, whatever that looks like for each of us.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Living by faith fuels our fire to run the race.<|5.50|> <|6.56|> It fuels our fire to show up, whether at a prayer vigil or in a polling place.<|13.66|> <|14.66|> Living by faith gives us the courage to kneel with our backs to an open door,<|20.86|> <|21.96|> believing that there are still places of sanctuary,<|25.24|> <|25.24|> sanctuary,<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and that fear and paranoia cannot and will not win.<|5.74|> <|6.88|> Living by faith invites us to use all of our senses.<|13.46|> <|15.36|> And living by faith ultimately means<|19.24|> <|19.24|> inviting the world in through our doors.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
